Thursday · June 19 · 2026 · Issue № 005

Front Page Essays Episodes Field Notes Archive About Subscribe
About · Established MMXXVI

Thinkers Dilemma is an introspective into the human existence — a newsletter and podcast at the intersection of technology, culture, diaspora experience, and systems thinking. For people who refuse comfortable answers.

iThe premise

There is an old question in moral philosophy — cui bono? — who benefits? It is the first question an investigator asks and the last one a marketing department wants answered. This project is built around asking it, out loud, of the systems we live inside.

We are not here for the outrage cycle. We are not interested in being first, or loudest, or most certain. We are interested in attention — the slow kind, the kind that survives a second reading. We would rather be late and right than early and forgettable.

This is writing for the curious lurker, the rabbit-hole researcher, the person who suspects the official story is incomplete and wants depth instead of heat. You are welcome here. You are especially welcome to disagree.

Question i
Who benefits?

Follow the value. The party that gains is rarely the party being asked to consent, and almost never the one footing the bill.

Question ii
Who pays?

And in what currency? The cost of a convenience is usually paid later, in data, in attention, in autonomy — not at the till.

Question iii
Who decides?

Defaults are decisions. Someone chose them, on your behalf, without a vote. We try to find out who, and why.

iiThe author

Thinkers Dilemma is written and produced by Taz Punjabi — a writer and thinker at the intersection of technology, culture, diaspora experience, and systems thinking.

The newsletter launched in May 2026. Each essay asks the same three standing questions of a different system: who benefits, who pays, and who decides. The answers are rarely comfortable. That is the point.

Every essay is published free on Substack. The podcast companion takes the written argument and makes it audible — the same inquiry, in the author's voice, at full length.

iiiThe method

Primary sources, read slowly and completely — including the documents nobody reads, which is precisely why we do. We trace value rather than villains; the interesting story is usually a system working exactly as designed, not a conspiracy.

We show the work. Numbers are artifacts, not decoration — word counts, dates, jurisdictions. When we are unsure, we say so. When we are wrong, we correct it in the open. The inquiry is collaborative — between the writer and the reader, between the question and the people generous enough to argue with it.

№ 03 How we publish Free · weekly
Weekly
The essay
Substack · Long-form

The written inquiry — argued at length, open to comment. Free to read, always.

Each essay
The episode
Podcast companion

The same argument made audible — the essay in the author's voice, at full length.

In between
Field notes
Key pullquotes

The sharpest sentence from each essay. Where the argument lands, clearly stated.

ivGet in touch

Tips, corrections, and arguments are welcome — the better the argument, the more welcome. Leave a comment directly on the essay at Substack, or reach out via the social links in the header.

For the newsletter and podcast, subscribe free at thinkersdilemma.substack.com. Every essay lands in your inbox the day it publishes.

Free · weekly

For people who refuse
comfortable answers.

1,840 readers · Unsubscribe anytime · No spam, ever
"
We are less interested in alarm than in attention. Read it. Sit with it. Disagree, openly.
Taz Punjabi, Thinkers Dilemma · On method
